title: Spring Boot Fundamentals
content: Reference for Spring Boot core concepts, configuration, lifecycle, and logging. Audience: intermediate to senior backend engineers.

sections.title: What is Spring Boot
sections.description: Core definition, characteristics, and positioning relative to the Spring Framework.

blocks.html:
Spring Boot is an opinionated extension of the Spring Framework that eliminates boilerplate configuration. It bundles an embedded server, sensible defaults, and auto-configuration so a Spring application starts with java -jar and no external container.

It does not replace Spring — it sits on top of it. Every Spring Boot application is a Spring application. The difference is in setup cost and operational posture.

paragraphs:
• Auto-configuration: Detects classpath libraries and configures beans automatically. Adding spring-boot-starter-data-jpa wires a DataSource, EntityManagerFactory, and transaction manager without a single XML file.
  • Embedded server: Tomcat, Jetty, or Undertow ships inside the JAR. No WAR deployment, no external container management.
  • Starter dependencies: Curated dependency sets. One starter pulls in a tested, compatible set of libraries at known versions.
  • Production-ready endpoints: Actuator exposes health, metrics, env, and thread dumps over HTTP or JMX out of the box.
  • No XML required: All configuration through annotations, application.properties, or application.yml.

Spring Boot applies sensible defaults for every configurable aspect — server port (8080), datasource URL structure, logging format, Jackson serialization behavior. You override only what diverges from the default.

This means configuration surface is proportional to how much you deviate from standard behavior. A typical microservice changes fewer than 20 properties. A legacy integration with non-standard JNDI datasources might touch many more.

"Opinionated" means Spring Boot makes choices on your behalf: Logback over Log4j2 by default, HikariCP as the connection pool, Jackson for JSON, Tomcat as the servlet container. These choices are reversible but require explicit opt-out.

The opinions exist because they represent the most commonly correct setup. Diverge when your constraints demand it — not to avoid the defaults.

sections.title: History of Spring Boot
sections.description: Origin and major milestones from 2013 to present.

blocks.html:
Spring Boot emerged from Pivotal in 2013 as a response to the configuration overhead of Spring MVC applications. Phil Webb and Dave Syer led the initial design. The primary goal was to make it possible to start a production-ready Spring application in under five minutes, without touching XML.

The project drew on lessons from Dropwizard and Play Framework — both of which popularized the embedded server + fat JAR pattern in the JVM ecosystem.

rows.highlights: Initial release. Auto-configuration, embedded Tomcat, starter POMs, Actuator.
Spring Framework 5, reactive stack (WebFlux), Actuator redesign, HTTP/2 support, Kotlin first-class.
Config file processing rewrite. Volume-mounted config for containers. Layered JARs.
Circular dependency detection enabled by default. SameSite cookie defaults.
Java 17 baseline. Jakarta EE 9+ (javax → jakarta). Native image support via GraalVM. Observability with Micrometer Tracing.
Virtual threads (Project Loom). RestClient. @ConditionalOnThreading. CDS support.
CDS auto-setup. Service connection improvements. Improved Docker Compose support.

paragraphs:
Boot 1.x established the core model: starter + auto-config + embedded server. Boot 2.x added the reactive programming model alongside the servlet stack, making both first-class. Boot 3.x shifted the baseline to Jakarta EE and made AOT compilation and native images viable for production.

The central pattern — @Conditional-based auto-configuration loaded via spring.factories (pre-3.x) or AutoConfiguration.imports (3.x+) — has not fundamentally changed. What changed is the performance envelope and the deployment targets.

sections.title: Why Spring Boot
sections.description: Advantages, trade-offs, and cases where Spring Boot is not the right fit.

blocks.html: Spring Boot's value is proportional to how standard your use case is. For greenfield microservices on a JVM stack, it compresses weeks of infrastructure wiring into hours. For highly specialized systems with non-standard runtime requirements, its defaults become obstacles.

paragraphs: Spring Initializr generates a runnable project in seconds. Starters eliminate dependency version conflicts. Auto-configuration wires infrastructure beans without manual @Bean declarations. A REST API with JPA persistence requires roughly 50 lines of application code — the framework handles the rest.

The application JAR includes the server. Deployment is java -jar. No container installation, no WAR packaging, no application server version coordination. Container images are simpler — base JRE image plus the fat JAR. This model aligns with Kubernetes, ECS, and most modern deployment targets.

Actuator exposes /actuator/health, /actuator/metrics, /actuator/env, and /actuator/threaddump without additional code. HikariCP provides connection pool metrics. Micrometer integrates with Prometheus, Datadog, and CloudWatch via one dependency. These capabilities require manual wiring in plain Spring.

Classpath scanning drives configuration. Adding spring-boot-starter-security activates HTTP Basic auth, CSRF protection, and session management — all overridable. Adding spring-boot-starter-cache wires a CacheManager. You configure only what the auto-config cannot infer from classpath and properties.

Spring Cloud builds on top of Boot to provide service discovery, config server, circuit breakers, and distributed tracing. Each microservice is an independent Boot application with its own embedded server and deployment artifact. Profile-based configuration handles environment differences without code changes.

Every major persistence layer (JPA, MongoDB, Redis, Cassandra, Elasticsearch), messaging system (Kafka, RabbitMQ, ActiveMQ), and cloud platform (AWS, GCP, Azure) has a Boot starter. Integration is one dependency and a properties block, not a custom integration layer.

blocks.subheading: When Not to Use Spring Boot
paragraphs:
• Startup time is critical: Boot's classpath scanning and auto-configuration add startup overhead. Native image (GraalVM) mitigates this but adds build complexity. Quarkus or Micronaut have faster cold starts on the JVM.
  • Memory footprint is severely constrained: A minimal Boot app consumes ~80–120MB heap at idle. Constrained environments (Raspberry Pi, edge devices) may need a lighter runtime.
  • Full control over container is required: Enterprise deployments managed by a separate ops team via JBoss/WebSphere may mandate WAR deployment. Boot supports this but it's not the natural fit.
  • Simple scripts or CLI tools: Boot's startup cost is unnecessary for short-lived processes. Plain Java or Picocli-based CLIs are more appropriate.

sections.title: Spring Boot vs Alternatives
sections.description: Feature and capability comparison across major JVM frameworks.

blocks.html: Framework selection depends on startup time requirements, ecosystem breadth, team familiarity, and operational model. The table below compares Spring Boot against Quarkus, Micronaut, and Vert.x across dimensions that matter at production scale.

paragraphs:
Spring Boot is the default choice when the team knows Spring, the service integrates with Spring Cloud, or broad library compatibility matters. Quarkus or Micronaut win when native image performance or sub-second cold starts are hard requirements. Vert.x fits high-concurrency event-driven pipelines where a full DI framework is unnecessary overhead.

The frameworks are not mutually exclusive in a system — a polyglot microservices architecture may run Boot services alongside Quarkus services without conflict.

sections.title: Project Setup
sections.description: Initializing a Spring Boot project, build tool structure, and minimal runnable application.

blocks.html: Spring Boot projects are created via Spring Initializr (start.spring.io) or IDE integration (IntelliJ, VS Code Spring Boot Extension). The output is a pre-configured Maven or Gradle project with the selected starters, a parent POM or BOM reference, and a runnable main class.

paragraphs:
• Project: Maven or Gradle (Groovy or Kotlin DSL).
  • Language: Java, Kotlin, or Groovy.
  • Spring Boot version: Selects the BOM and auto-config versions. Prefer the latest GA release.
  • Group / Artifact / Name: Sets the Maven coordinates and package structure.
  • Packaging: JAR (default, embedded server) or WAR (external container).
  • Java version: Spring Boot 3.x requires Java 17+. Boot 2.x supports Java 8+.
  • Dependencies: Each selected dependency maps to one or more starters added to the build file.

blocks.title: Standard Project Structure
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid diagram showing the standard Spring Boot Maven project directory tree.

Use a `graph TD` (top-down tree) layout.

Nodes to include (as a directory tree):
- demo/ (root)
  - src/
    - main/
      - java/com/example/demo/
        - DemoApplication.java
        - controller/
        - service/
        - repository/
        - model/
      - resources/
        - application.yml
        - static/
        - templates/
    - test/
      - java/com/example/demo/
        - DemoApplicationTests.java
  - pom.xml (or build.gradle)
  - target/ (or build/)

Style:
- Use rectangular nodes for directories, rounded nodes for files
- Group src/main and src/test visually
- Label each terminal node (file) with a brief annotation in parentheses
- No color fills — monochrome, clean

rows.purpose: Application source code. Package mirrors Group + Artifact ID.
Configuration files, static assets, templates. On classpath at runtime.
Primary configuration file. Loaded automatically by Spring Boot.
Served as static content under / by the embedded web server.
Thymeleaf / Mustache / FreeMarker templates. Resolved by view resolver.
Test sources. @SpringBootTest loads the full context here.
Compiled output. mvn package produces the fat JAR here.

sections.title: @SpringBootApplication
sections.description: Composition, attributes, and behavioral implications of the @SpringBootApplication annotation.

blocks.html: @SpringBootApplication is a composed annotation. It combines three annotations into one: @SpringBootConfiguration, @EnableAutoConfiguration, and @ComponentScan. Placing it on the main class is the standard entry point for auto-configuration and component scanning.

paragraphs:
• @SpringBootConfiguration: Specialization of @Configuration. Marks the class as a source of bean definitions. Only one per application context.
  • @EnableAutoConfiguration: Triggers auto-configuration. Spring Boot reads META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports and conditionally activates the listed configuration classes.
  • @ComponentScan: Scans the package of the annotated class and all sub-packages for Spring-managed components (@Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller, etc.).

rows.description: Auto-configuration classes to exclude. Equivalent to @EnableAutoConfiguration(exclude=...).
Class names to exclude when the class is not on the classpath.
Overrides the default component scan root packages.
Type-safe alternative to scanBasePackages. Uses package of the specified classes.
Custom bean name generator for scanned components.
If false, disables CGLIB proxying of @Bean methods. Reduces startup time; @Bean inter-calls won't be intercepted.

blocks.title: @SpringBootApplication Composition
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid diagram showing the composition of @SpringBootApplication.

Use `graph TD` layout.

Structure:
- Top node: `@SpringBootApplication`
- Three child nodes (connected with arrows from top node):
  1. `@SpringBootConfiguration` → child: `@Configuration`
  2. `@EnableAutoConfiguration` → annotation behavior note: "Reads AutoConfiguration.imports, activates @Conditional beans"
  3. `@ComponentScan` → annotation behavior note: "Scans base package + sub-packages for @Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller"
- For `@SpringBootConfiguration` → `@Configuration` → note: "Source of @Bean definitions"

Style:
- Rectangular nodes for annotations
- Rounded note nodes for explanations
- Monochrome, no color fills
- Arrows labeled: `composes`

paragraphs:
At startup, @EnableAutoConfiguration loads all class names from META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports (Boot 3.x) or META-INF/spring.factories (Boot 2.x). Each listed class is evaluated against its @Conditional conditions — classpath presence, bean existence, property values. Only classes whose conditions pass are registered in the application context.

Setting --debug or logging.level.org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure=DEBUG prints a CONDITIONS EVALUATION REPORT that lists every auto-configuration class and whether it matched or was excluded.

sections.title: SpringApplication Class
sections.description: Constructors, static methods, instance configuration methods, and SpringApplicationBuilder.

blocks.html: SpringApplication is the bootstrap class. SpringApplication.run() is sufficient for most applications. Direct instantiation provides control over banner mode, web application type, listeners, and initial properties before the context is created.

rows.description: Standard constructor. Takes one or more configuration classes or component-scanned root classes.
Allows injecting a custom ResourceLoader — useful in OSGi environments or when loading resources from a non-standard location.
Bootstraps the application from a single primary source.
Bootstraps from multiple primary sources.
Closes the context and returns an exit code. Pass to System.exit().
Controls startup banner: CONSOLE, LOG, or OFF.
Overrides auto-detected type: SERVLET, REACTIVE, or NONE.
Registers listeners for application events fired before context refresh.
Lowest-priority properties. Overridden by all other sources.
Registers context initializers that run before context refresh.
Additional configuration sources (class names, packages, XML) beyond primary sources.
If true, beans are created on first access rather than at startup. Reduces startup time; defers errors.
Sets java.awt.headless system property. Default: true.

blocks.code:
new SpringApplicationBuilder(Application.class)
    .bannerMode(Banner.Mode.OFF)
    .web(WebApplicationType.SERVLET)
    .profiles("prod")
    .properties("server.port=8443")
    .listeners(new ApplicationPidFileWriter())
    .run(args);

// Parent-child context hierarchy
new SpringApplicationBuilder(ParentConfig.class)
    .child(ChildWebConfig.class)
    .web(WebApplicationType.SERVLET)
    .run(args);

sections.title: Auto-Configuration Internals
sections.description: How Spring Boot discovers, evaluates, and applies auto-configuration classes.

blocks.html:
Auto-configuration is the mechanism that eliminates explicit @Bean declarations for infrastructure components. It works by loading a list of candidate configuration classes and evaluating each against a set of @Conditional conditions at context refresh time. Classes whose conditions pass are registered; others are skipped entirely.

Understanding this mechanism is essential for debugging unexpected beans, writing custom starters, and excluding conflicting auto-configurations.

blocks.title: Auto-Configuration Evaluation Flow
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid flowchart showing the Spring Boot auto-configuration evaluation process.

Use `flowchart TD` layout.

Steps in order:
1. `Application Startup` → triggers `@EnableAutoConfiguration`
2. `@EnableAutoConfiguration` → reads `AutoConfiguration.imports` (Boot 3.x) or `spring.factories` (Boot 2.x)
3. From the file → loads list of candidate `AutoConfiguration` classes
4. For each candidate → evaluate `@Conditional` annotations:
   - `@ConditionalOnClass` — is the class on the classpath?
   - `@ConditionalOnMissingBean` — does the bean already exist?
   - `@ConditionalOnProperty` — is the property set to expected value?
5. If ALL conditions pass → register configuration class → instantiate `@Bean` definitions
6. If ANY condition fails → skip class entirely
7. Final output → beans registered in `ApplicationContext`

Add a side node: `--debug flag` → prints CONDITIONS EVALUATION REPORT

Style:
- Decision diamonds for conditional checks
- Rectangular boxes for actions
- Monochrome

paragraphs:
• Boot 3.x: Auto-configuration classes are listed in META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports, one fully-qualified class name per line. This file ships inside each starter JAR.
  • Boot 2.x: Same list lives in META-INF/spring.factories under the key org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.EnableAutoConfiguration.
  • Loading order: All candidate classes from all JARs on the classpath are collected first, then evaluated in a defined order influenced by @AutoConfigureBefore, @AutoConfigureAfter, and @AutoConfigureOrder.

Each line in META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports is a fully-qualified class name. The class must be annotated with @AutoConfiguration (Boot 3.x) or @Configuration (Boot 2.x). Spring Boot reads all such files from all JARs on the classpath and merges them into a single candidate list before evaluation begins.

To register a custom auto-configuration, create this file in your JAR's META-INF/spring/ directory with your configuration class name.

• @ConditionalOnClass: Activates only if the specified class is on the classpath. Used to gate database configs on the presence of driver classes.
  • @ConditionalOnMissingClass: Inverse — activates only if the class is absent.
  • @ConditionalOnBean: Activates only if a matching bean already exists in the context.
  • @ConditionalOnMissingBean: Most common — activates only if no matching bean exists. Allows user-defined beans to override auto-configured ones.
  • @ConditionalOnProperty: Activates based on a property value. Supports havingValue, matchIfMissing.
  • @ConditionalOnWebApplication: Activates only in a servlet or reactive web context.
  • @ConditionalOnExpression: Evaluates a SpEL expression. Use sparingly — harder to debug.
  • @ConditionalOnResource: Activates if a classpath resource exists.

• @AutoConfigureBefore(X.class): Ensures this configuration is evaluated before X. Does not guarantee X runs — X may still be excluded.
  • @AutoConfigureAfter(X.class): Evaluated after X. Use when your config depends on beans from X being present.
  • @AutoConfigureOrder(n): Numeric ordering within the same phase. Lower values run first. Rarely needed — prefer Before/After.

blocks.code:
# Option 1: JVM argument
java -jar app.jar --debug

# Option 2: application.yml
# debug: true

# Option 3: environment variable
DEBUG=true java -jar app.jar

# Output includes:
# Positive matches  — conditions that passed
# Negative matches  — conditions that failed (with reason)
# Exclusions        — explicitly excluded classes
# Unconditional     — always-active configurations

sections.title: Spring Boot Starters
sections.description: Starter dependency model, official starters catalog, and custom starter structure.

blocks.html: A starter is a Maven/Gradle dependency that pulls in a curated, version-compatible set of libraries and activates the corresponding auto-configuration. You add one dependency; Spring Boot wires the infrastructure. Starters do not contain application code — they are pure dependency aggregators with an optional auto-configuration JAR.

blocks.title: Starter Dependency Resolution
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid diagram showing how a Spring Boot starter resolves to running beans.

Use `graph LR` (left-to-right) layout.

Flow:
1. Developer adds `spring-boot-starter-data-jpa` to `pom.xml`
2. Maven resolves transitive dependencies:
   - `spring-data-jpa`
   - `hibernate-core`
   - `spring-jdbc`
   - `HikariCP`
   - `spring-orm`
3. `JpaRepositoriesAutoConfiguration` is on the classpath
4. `@ConditionalOnClass(JpaRepository.class)` → passes
5. `@ConditionalOnMissingBean(JpaRepositoryFactoryBean.class)` → passes
6. Spring Boot registers:
   - `DataSource` (HikariCP)
   - `EntityManagerFactory`
   - `TransactionManager`
   - `JpaRepositories`

Style:
- Group the transitive dependencies in a subgraph labeled `Transitive Dependencies`
- Group the registered beans in a subgraph labeled `Auto-Configured Beans`
- Monochrome, rectangular nodes

paragraphs:
A starter JAR typically contains only a pom.xml (dependency aggregation) and no Java source. A separate -autoconfigure JAR contains the actual auto-configuration classes and the AutoConfiguration.imports file. This separation allows consumers to use the auto-configuration without the starter's opinionated dependency set, or to use the dependencies without activating auto-configuration.

Example: spring-boot-starter-data-jpa depends on spring-boot-autoconfigure, which contains JpaRepositoriesAutoConfiguration.

sections.title: Application Lifecycle
sections.description: Startup sequence, lifecycle events, runner interfaces, and shutdown hooks.

blocks.html: Spring Boot's startup sequence is deterministic. Understanding the order of events matters when running initialization logic, registering beans programmatically, or diagnosing startup failures. Shutdown is equally structured — beans are destroyed in reverse creation order, and graceful shutdown waits for in-flight requests before stopping.

blocks.title: Application Lifecycle Sequence
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid sequence diagram showing the Spring Boot application startup and shutdown sequence.

Participants:
- `main()` (entry point)
- `SpringApplication`
- `ApplicationContext`
- `ApplicationListeners`
- `Beans`
- `EmbeddedServer`

Startup sequence:
1. `main()` → `SpringApplication.run()`
2. `SpringApplication` → fires `ApplicationStartingEvent` → `ApplicationListeners`
3. `SpringApplication` → prepares `Environment` → fires `ApplicationEnvironmentPreparedEvent`
4. `SpringApplication` → creates `ApplicationContext`
5. `SpringApplication` → fires `ApplicationContextInitializedEvent`
6. `SpringApplication` → loads bean definitions → `ApplicationContext`
7. `SpringApplication` → fires `ApplicationPreparedEvent`
8. `ApplicationContext` → `refresh()` → instantiates all `Beans`
9. `ApplicationContext` → `Beans` (calls `@PostConstruct` methods)
10. `SpringApplication` → starts `EmbeddedServer`
11. `SpringApplication` → runs `CommandLineRunner` / `ApplicationRunner` → `Beans`
12. `SpringApplication` → fires `ApplicationReadyEvent`

Shutdown sequence (add below with separator):
1. JVM shutdown signal → `SpringApplication` shutdown hook
2. `EmbeddedServer` stops accepting new requests (graceful shutdown)
3. `ApplicationContext.close()`
4. `Beans` — calls `@PreDestroy` methods in reverse order
5. Context closed

Style: Standard Mermaid sequence diagram, monochrome

rows.when: After SpringApplication.run() is called, before anything else
After Environment is ready, before context creation
After context created and initializers called, before bean definitions loaded
After bean definitions loaded, before context refresh
After context refresh completes. All beans instantiated.
After embedded server starts. Port is accessible.
After context refresh, before runners execute
After runners complete. Application is ready to serve traffic.
If startup fails at any stage

paragraphs: Implement CommandLineRunner to run code after the application context is fully started. Receives raw String[] command-line arguments. Runs before ApplicationReadyEvent. Multiple runners execute in @Order sequence.

Same as CommandLineRunner but receives an ApplicationArguments object instead of raw strings. ApplicationArguments parses --key=value pairs into named options and positional arguments separately. Use this when the application logic needs to distinguish between option arguments and non-option arguments.

• @PostConstruct: Called after dependency injection, before the bean is available to other beans. Avoid heavy blocking work here — it holds up context refresh.
  • @PreDestroy: Called during context shutdown before the bean is removed. Use to release resources (connections, thread pools, file handles).
  • InitializingBean / DisposableBean: Interface-based alternative. afterPropertiesSet() and destroy(). Less common — @PostConstruct/@PreDestroy are preferred.
  • @Bean(initMethod/destroyMethod): Specify method names on third-party beans where you can't add annotations to the class.

Spring Boot includes FailureAnalyzer implementations that translate common startup exceptions into human-readable messages with suggested fixes. Examples: port already in use, missing required property, datasource connection failure.

Custom FailureAnalyzer implementations can be registered in META-INF/spring.factories under org.springframework.boot.diagnostics.FailureAnalyzer. Each analyzer receives the exception and returns a FailureAnalysis with a description and action string, or null if it doesn't handle that exception type.

sections.title: Embedded Server
sections.description: Server selection, configuration, SSL setup, and programmatic customization.

blocks.html:
Spring Boot embeds a servlet container (Tomcat, Jetty, or Undertow) or a reactive server (Reactor Netty) in the application JAR. The server starts and stops with the application. No external container installation or WAR deployment is required.

Tomcat is the default for spring-boot-starter-web. Reactor Netty is the default for spring-boot-starter-webflux. Switching servers requires excluding the default and adding the replacement.

blocks.title: Embedded Server Architecture
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid diagram showing how an embedded server fits inside a Spring Boot application JAR.

Use `graph TD` layout.

Top level: `Fat JAR (app.jar)`
Inside the JAR (subgraph):
- `Application Code` (controllers, services, repositories)
- `Spring Framework` (IoC container, MVC, Data)
- `Spring Boot` (auto-config, actuator, starters)
- `Embedded Server` with three options shown as sub-nodes:
  - `Tomcat` (default for servlet)
  - `Jetty` (alternative)
  - `Undertow` (alternative)
  - `Reactor Netty` (default for reactive / WebFlux)

External to JAR:
- `JVM` wraps the fat JAR
- `OS` wraps the JVM
- `HTTP Client` sends requests to `Embedded Server`

Style:
- Use a subgraph for `Fat JAR` contents
- Monochrome
- Arrow from `HTTP Client` → `Embedded Server` labeled `HTTP/HTTPS`

blocks.code:
server:
  port: 8080
  address: 0.0.0.0          # bind address
  shutdown: graceful        # wait for in-flight requests on shutdown

  tomcat:
    threads:
      max: 200              # max worker threads (default: 200)
      min-spare: 10         # min idle threads
    connection-timeout: 20s
    max-connections: 8192
    accept-count: 100       # queue size when all threads busy
    max-swallow-size: 2MB   # max request body to swallow on error
    uri-encoding: UTF-8

  compression:
    enabled: true
    mime-types: application/json,text/html
    min-response-size: 1024

<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
  <exclusions>
    <exclusion>
      <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
      <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-tomcat</artifactId>
    </exclusion>
  </exclusions>
</dependency>

<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-jetty</artifactId>
</dependency>

<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
  <exclusions>
    <exclusion>
      <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
      <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-tomcat</artifactId>
    </exclusion>
  </exclusions>
</dependency>

<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-undertow</artifactId>
</dependency>

server:
  port: 8443
  ssl:
    enabled: true
    key-store: classpath:keystore.p12
    key-store-password: changeit
    key-store-type: PKCS12
    key-alias: myapp
    # Restrict to TLS 1.2+
    protocol: TLS
    enabled-protocols: TLSv1.2,TLSv1.3
    # Mutual TLS (client certificate auth)
    # client-auth: need
    # trust-store: classpath:truststore.p12
    # trust-store-password: changeit

blocks.html:
Spring Boot resolves configuration from multiple sources in a defined priority order. Higher-priority sources override lower-priority ones. The same property key resolves to exactly one value — the one from the highest-priority source that defines it.

The two primary binding mechanisms are @ConfigurationProperties (type-safe, validated, bulk binding) and @Value (inline injection, SpEL support, individual properties). Use @ConfigurationProperties for structured configuration blocks; use @Value for single injected values.

blocks.title: Configuration Source Precedence
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid diagram showing Spring Boot's configuration source precedence from highest to lowest priority.

Use `graph TD` (top = highest priority, bottom = lowest).

Sources in order (top to bottom, each as a node):
1. Command-line arguments (`--server.port=9090`)
2. `SPRING_APPLICATION_JSON` (inline JSON in env var or system property)
3. OS environment variables (`SERVER_PORT`)
4. Java system properties (`-Dserver.port=9090`)
5. `@TestPropertySource` (test-only)
6. Devtools global settings (~/.spring-boot-devtools.properties)
7. `application-{profile}.yml` / `.properties` (profile-specific, outside JAR)
8. `application-{profile}.yml` / `.properties` (profile-specific, inside JAR)
9. `application.yml` / `.properties` (outside JAR)
10. `application.yml` / `.properties` (inside JAR)
11. `@PropertySource` on `@Configuration` classes
12. Default properties (`SpringApplication.setDefaultProperties`)

Connections: linear arrows from top to bottom labeled `overrides`

Style:
- Highlight nodes 1-3 with a note: `runtime overrides`
- Highlight nodes 9-10 with a note: `standard config`
- Monochrome otherwise

files.code:
spring.application.name=my-service
server.port=8080
server.shutdown=graceful

spring.datasource.url=jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/mydb
spring.datasource.username=app_user
spring.datasource.password=${DB_PASSWORD}
spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=20
spring.datasource.hikari.minimum-idle=5

spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=validate
spring.jpa.show-sql=false
spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.dialect=org.hibernate.dialect.PostgreSQLDialect

logging.level.com.example=DEBUG
logging.level.org.springframework.web=INFO

spring:
  application:
    name: my-service
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/mydb
    username: app_user
    password: ${DB_PASSWORD}
    hikari:
      maximum-pool-size: 20
      minimum-idle: 5
  jpa:
    hibernate:
      ddl-auto: validate
    show-sql: false
    properties:
      hibernate:
        dialect: org.hibernate.dialect.PostgreSQLDialect

server:
  port: 8080
  shutdown: graceful

logging:
  level:
    com.example: DEBUG
    org.springframework.web: INFO

rows.description: Service name. Used by actuator, tracing, and service discovery.
HTTP port. Set to 0 for random port (useful in tests).
Set to graceful to wait for in-flight requests before shutdown.
Comma-separated list of active profiles.
JDBC URL. Drives DataSource auto-configuration.
Schema generation: none, validate, update, create, create-drop.
Delay bean creation until first use. Faster startup, deferred errors.
Per-package log level. E.g. logging.level.com.example=DEBUG.
Actuator endpoints to expose over HTTP. Use * for all.
Import additional config files or config servers. E.g. configserver:.

blocks.html: @ConfigurationProperties binds a prefix namespace from the application configuration to a Java class. All fields in the class are populated from properties matching prefix.fieldName. Supports nested objects, lists, maps, and JSR-380 validation. Prefix binding uses relaxed binding — myApp.baseUrl, my-app.base-url, and MY_APP_BASE_URL all map to the same field.

rows.description: Property namespace to bind. Use kebab-case: my-service.
Alias for prefix.
If false, unknown property keys under the prefix throw a binding exception.
If true, type conversion errors are ignored. Not recommended for required config.

blocks.code:
app:
  name: my-service
  database:
    url: jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/mydb
    max-pool-size: 20

  # List binding
  allowed-origins:
    - https://app.example.com
    - https://admin.example.com

  # Map binding
  feature-flags:
    dark-mode: true
    beta-api: false

blocks.html: @Value injects a single property value or SpEL expression into a field, constructor parameter, or method parameter. Simpler than @ConfigurationProperties for isolated values, but provides no validation, no relaxed binding, and no IDE metadata support. Use for truly standalone values; use @ConfigurationProperties for any group of related settings.

rows.resolves: Property value, or literal default if absent
SpEL expression evaluated at injection time
Result of method call on a Spring bean

sections.title: Profiles
sections.description: Profile-specific configuration, @Profile annotation, activation mechanisms, and profile groups.

blocks.html:
Profiles segregate configuration and bean definitions by environment (dev, staging, prod) or feature set. An active profile activates profile-specific property files and @Profile-annotated beans. Multiple profiles can be active simultaneously.

Profiles are not security boundaries — they are a configuration management mechanism. Sensitive values (credentials, API keys) belong in environment variables or a secrets manager regardless of profile.

blocks.title: Profile Resolution
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid diagram showing how Spring Boot resolves configuration when multiple profiles are active.

Use `graph TD` layout.

Scenario: active profiles are `prod` and `metrics`.

Base configuration sources (merged, lowest to highest):
1. `application.yml` (base config)
2. `application-prod.yml` (profile-specific, overrides base)
3. `application-metrics.yml` (profile-specific, overrides base)
4. Environment variables (highest priority)

Show:
- Each source as a node
- Arrows from lower to higher priority labeled `overrides`
- A final node `Resolved Environment` that all sources feed into
- A side note: `Last profile in spring.profiles.active list wins for same-key conflicts`

Style: monochrome, clean, left-to-right or top-down

blocks.code:
# application.yml — base configuration
spring:
  application:
    name: my-service
server:
  port: 8080

---
# Activated when spring.profiles.active=dev
spring:
  config:
    activate:
      on-profile: dev
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:h2:mem:devdb
    driver-class-name: org.h2.Driver
logging:
  level:
    com.example: DEBUG

---
# Activated when spring.profiles.active=prod
spring:
  config:
    activate:
      on-profile: prod
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:postgresql://${DB_HOST}:5432/mydb
    username: ${DB_USER}
    password: ${DB_PASSWORD}
logging:
  level:
    com.example: WARN

files.code:
# Activate at config level (lowest priority — usually not done in base file)
spring:
  profiles:
    active: dev

# Environment variable (recommended for containers)
export SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod,metrics
java -jar app.jar

# Or as JVM argument
java -jar app.jar --spring.profiles.active=prod,metrics

# Or as system property
java -Dspring.profiles.active=prod -jar app.jar

blocks.code:
# application.yml
# Activating 'prod' also activates 'prod-db', 'prod-cache', and 'prod-monitoring'
spring:
  profiles:
    group:
      prod:
        - prod-db
        - prod-cache
        - prod-monitoring
      dev:
        - dev-db
        - dev-stubs

sections.title: Environment Abstraction
sections.description: The Environment interface, property sources, and custom property source registration.

blocks.html: Environment is Spring's unified interface for accessing property values and active profiles. It aggregates all property sources — system properties, environment variables, application config files, @PropertySource — into a single query interface. Inject it directly when you need programmatic property access or profile checks.

rows.description: Returns property value or null if not found.
Returns property value or the specified default.
Returns property converted to the target type.
Returns property value; throws IllegalStateException if absent.
True if the key exists in any property source.
Currently active profile names.
Default profiles (active when no explicit profiles are set).
Evaluates profile expressions (&, |, !). Boot 2.4+.
Resolves ${placeholder} expressions in the given string.
JVM -D arguments and System.getProperties().
OS env vars. Dot notation converted from underscore: SERVER_PORT → server.port.
Values from application.yml / application.properties.
--key=value args passed to main().
User-defined source. Registered via ApplicationContextInitializer or EnvironmentPostProcessor.

sections.title: Logging
sections.description: Default logging stack, Logback configuration, profile-based logging, Log4j2, structured logging, Kafka appender, ELK integration, Loki/Grafana, distributed tracing correlation, and best practices.

blocks.html:
Spring Boot uses SLF4J as the logging facade and Logback as the default implementation. All Spring Framework internal logging routes through SLF4J. Application code logs through SLF4J — the underlying framework is swappable without changing application code.

Log configuration spans four concerns: output format, log levels, appender targets (console, file, Kafka, remote), and structured output for log aggregation pipelines.

blocks.title: Spring Boot Logging Architecture
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid diagram showing the Spring Boot logging architecture.

Use `graph LR` (left-to-right) layout.

Layers (left to right):
1. `Application Code` → calls `SLF4J API (Logger)`
2. `SLF4J API` → bridges to one of:
   - `Logback` (default, via logback-classic)
   - `Log4j2` (optional, via log4j2 bridge)
   - `JUL` (java.util.logging, via jul-to-slf4j)
3. From `Logback` → multiple appenders:
   - `ConsoleAppender` → stdout
   - `RollingFileAppender` → log files
   - `KafkaAppender` → Kafka topic
   - `LogstashEncoder` → JSON format → Filebeat → Logstash → Elasticsearch
4. From `Log4j2` → similar appenders

Show bridge adapters between JUL/Log4j2 legacy and SLF4J with dashed lines.

Style: monochrome, clear groupings, no color fills

blocks.html: Without any Logback configuration file, Spring Boot applies its own default configuration: console output, pattern format, INFO level for all loggers. This is sufficient for development and container environments where stdout is captured by the orchestrator.

paragraphs:
• Facade: SLF4J — all application and framework logging goes through this API.
  • Implementation: Logback (logback-classic) — included transitively via spring-boot-starter-logging, which is in every starter.
  • JCL bridge: jcl-over-slf4j — routes Apache Commons Logging to SLF4J.
  • JUL bridge: jul-to-slf4j — routes java.util.logging to SLF4J.
  • Log4j bridge: log4j-over-slf4j — routes Log4j 1.x to SLF4J.

rows.use_for: Fine-grained execution tracing. Never in production.
Diagnostic info useful during development. Disable in prod unless investigating.
Normal operational events: startup, request handling, state transitions.
Unexpected conditions that don't fail the operation but require attention.
Failures that affect operation. Always investigate.
Suppresses all output for the logger.

blocks.code:
# Default pattern:
# DATE       TIME         LEVEL   PID     ---    THREAD          LOGGER                          MESSAGE
2024-01-15 10:23:45.123  INFO  12345 --- [main]         com.example.app.Application      : Started Application in 2.345 seconds
2024-01-15 10:23:46.456  DEBUG 12345 --- [http-nio-8080-exec-1] c.e.app.controller.UserController : GET /users/42
2024-01-15 10:23:46.789  ERROR 12345 --- [http-nio-8080-exec-1] c.e.app.service.UserService       : User 42 not found

# Color output is enabled by default on terminals that support ANSI escape codes

logging:
  level:
    root: INFO                          # global default
    com.example: DEBUG                  # all classes in package
    com.example.service.PaymentService: TRACE  # specific class
    org.springframework.web: DEBUG      # Spring MVC request logging
    org.hibernate.SQL: DEBUG            # SQL statements
    org.hibernate.type.descriptor.sql: TRACE  # SQL bind parameters
    org.springframework.security: DEBUG # Spring Security decisions
  
  # Log file output (in addition to console)
  file:
    name: /var/log/myapp/application.log
  
  # Log pattern override
  pattern:
    console: "%d{yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss} %-5level [%thread] %logger{36} - %msg%n"
    file: "%d{yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss} %-5level [%thread] %logger{36} - %msg%n"

blocks.html: For production logging, define a logback-spring.xml in src/main/resources. This file is processed by Spring Boot before Logback initializes, enabling access to Spring's <springProfile> and <springProperty> extensions. A plain logback.xml is processed by Logback directly and cannot use these extensions.

paragraphs:
• logback-spring.xml: Processed by Spring Boot. Supports <springProfile> for profile-conditional config and <springProperty> to read application properties. Use this in Boot applications.
  • logback.xml: Processed by Logback directly, before Spring initializes. Cannot use Spring extensions. Required only when logging must be configured before Spring context starts.

rows.role: Named logging channel. Hierarchy based on package: com.example is parent of com.example.service.
Output destination: console, file, socket, Kafka. A logger can have multiple appenders.
Converts log events to bytes. PatternLayoutEncoder for text, LogstashEncoder for JSON.
Formats the log event as a string. Used inside encoders.
Accepts or rejects log events before they reach the appender. E.g. ThresholdFilter, MDCFilter.

blocks.code:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<configuration>

  <!-- Read property from Spring environment -->
  <springProperty name="appName" source="spring.application.name" defaultValue="app"/>

  <!-- Console appender -->
  <appender name="CONSOLE" class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
    <encoder>
      <pattern>%d{yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss.SSS} %-5level [%thread] [${appName}] %logger{36} - %msg%n</pattern>
      <charset>UTF-8</charset>
    </encoder>
  </appender>

  <!-- Root logger -->
  <root level="INFO">
    <appender-ref ref="CONSOLE"/>
  </root>

  <!-- Package-level overrides -->
  <logger name="com.example" level="DEBUG"/>
  <logger name="org.springframework.web" level="INFO"/>
  <logger name="org.hibernate.SQL" level="DEBUG"/>

</configuration>

<appender name="FILE" class="ch.qos.logback.core.rolling.RollingFileAppender">
  <file>/var/log/myapp/application.log</file>

  <rollingPolicy class="ch.qos.logback.core.rolling.TimeBasedRollingPolicy">
    <!-- Daily rotation -->
    <fileNamePattern>/var/log/myapp/application.%d{yyyy-MM-dd}.%i.log.gz</fileNamePattern>
    <!-- Keep 30 days, max 3GB total -->
    <maxHistory>30</maxHistory>
    <totalSizeCap>3GB</totalSizeCap>
    <timeBasedFileNamingAndTriggeringPolicy
        class="ch.qos.logback.core.rolling.SizeAndTimeBasedFNATP">
      <maxFileSize>100MB</maxFileSize>
    </timeBasedFileNamingAndTriggeringPolicy>
  </rollingPolicy>

  <encoder>
    <pattern>%d{yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss.SSS} %-5level [%thread] %logger{36} - %msg%n</pattern>
  </encoder>
</appender>

<!-- Dependency: net.logstash.logback:logstash-logback-encoder -->
<appender name="JSON_CONSOLE" class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
  <encoder class="net.logstash.logback.encoder.LogstashEncoder">
    <!-- Include MDC fields in output -->
    <includeMdcKeyName>traceId</includeMdcKeyName>
    <includeMdcKeyName>spanId</includeMdcKeyName>
    <includeMdcKeyName>userId</includeMdcKeyName>
    <!-- Add static fields -->
    <customFields>{"service":"my-service","env":"prod"}</customFields>
    <!-- Exclude verbose fields -->
    <fieldNames>
      <timestamp>@timestamp</timestamp>
      <message>message</message>
      <logger>logger</logger>
      <thread>thread</thread>
      <level>level</level>
    </fieldNames>
  </encoder>
</appender>

<!-- Wraps any appender to make it non-blocking -->
<!-- Log events are queued; a separate thread handles I/O -->
<appender name="ASYNC_FILE" class="ch.qos.logback.classic.AsyncAppender">
  <!-- Queue capacity — increase for bursty workloads -->
  <queueSize>512</queueSize>
  <!-- Drop events when queue > 80% full (0 = never drop) -->
  <discardingThreshold>0</discardingThreshold>
  <!-- Wait up to 1000ms for queue to drain on shutdown -->
  <maxFlushTime>1000</maxFlushTime>
  <!-- Whether to include caller data (expensive) -->
  <includeCallerData>false</includeCallerData>
  <appender-ref ref="FILE"/>
</appender>

<root level="INFO">
  <appender-ref ref="ASYNC_FILE"/>
</root>

paragraphs:
Logback uses a logger hierarchy. A logger named com.example.service inherits the level of com.example if its own level is not set. The root logger is the ultimate ancestor. Appender additivity — enabled by default — means a logger sends events to its own appenders AND its parent's appenders. Set additivity="false" on a logger to prevent event propagation to parent appenders.

Configuration is reloaded when <configuration scan="true" scanPeriod="30 seconds"> is set. Logback polls the file; changes apply without restart. Useful in environments where log levels need runtime adjustment without a config server.

blocks.html: Log configuration often differs by environment: DEBUG to console in dev, INFO as JSON to a file in prod. logback-spring.xml supports the <springProfile> element to activate appenders or loggers conditionally on the active Spring profile.

paragraphs:
The <springProfile name="..."/> element wraps any Logback configuration. Its name attribute supports the same expressions as @Profile: a single name, a comma-separated list (OR logic), or ! for negation. The enclosed configuration is only applied when the profile condition matches.

Profile-specific appender selection and logger levels are the most common use cases.

blocks.code:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<configuration>

  <appender name="CONSOLE" class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
    <encoder>
      <pattern>%d{HH:mm:ss} %-5level [%thread] %logger{36} - %msg%n</pattern>
    </encoder>
  </appender>

  <appender name="JSON_CONSOLE" class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
    <encoder class="net.logstash.logback.encoder.LogstashEncoder"/>
  </appender>

  <!-- Development: plain text, DEBUG level -->
  <springProfile name="dev">
    <root level="DEBUG">
      <appender-ref ref="CONSOLE"/>
    </root>
    <logger name="org.springframework" level="INFO"/>
  </springProfile>

  <!-- Production: JSON output, INFO level -->
  <springProfile name="prod">
    <root level="INFO">
      <appender-ref ref="JSON_CONSOLE"/>
    </root>
  </springProfile>

  <!-- Test: suppress most output -->
  <springProfile name="test">
    <root level="WARN">
      <appender-ref ref="CONSOLE"/>
    </root>
  </springProfile>

</configuration>

# application.yml — base
logging:
  level:
    root: INFO
    com.example: INFO

---
spring:
  config:
    activate:
      on-profile: dev
logging:
  level:
    root: DEBUG
    com.example: TRACE
    org.springframework.web.servlet.DispatcherServlet: DEBUG

---
spring:
  config:
    activate:
      on-profile: prod
logging:
  level:
    root: WARN
    com.example: INFO

blocks.html: Log4j2 is an alternative to Logback with better async performance and a plugin-based architecture. Switching requires excluding spring-boot-starter-logging (which includes Logback) and adding spring-boot-starter-log4j2. Configuration is in log4j2-spring.xml or log4j2.xml.

blocks.code:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
  <exclusions>
    <exclusion>
      <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
      <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-logging</artifactId>
    </exclusion>
  </exclusions>
</dependency>

<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-log4j2</artifactId>
</dependency>

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<Configuration status="WARN" monitorInterval="30">

  <Properties>
    <Property name="LOG_PATTERN">%d{yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss.SSS} %-5level [%t] %logger{36} - %msg%n</Property>
  </Properties>

  <Appenders>
    <Console name="CONSOLE" target="SYSTEM_OUT">
      <PatternLayout pattern="${LOG_PATTERN}"/>
    </Console>

    <RollingFile name="FILE"
                 fileName="/var/log/myapp/application.log"
                 filePattern="/var/log/myapp/application.%d{yyyy-MM-dd}.%i.log.gz">
      <PatternLayout pattern="${LOG_PATTERN}"/>
      <Policies>
        <TimeBasedTriggeringPolicy/>
        <SizeBasedTriggeringPolicy size="100MB"/>
      </Policies>
      <DefaultRolloverStrategy max="30"/>
    </RollingFile>
  </Appenders>

  <Loggers>
    <Logger name="com.example" level="DEBUG" additivity="false">
      <AppenderRef ref="CONSOLE"/>
    </Logger>
    <Root level="INFO">
      <AppenderRef ref="CONSOLE"/>
      <AppenderRef ref="FILE"/>
    </Root>
  </Loggers>

</Configuration>

<!-- Requires: com.lmax:disruptor dependency -->
<!-- Set system property: log4j2.contextSelector=
     org.apache.logging.log4j.core.async.AsyncLoggerContextSelector -->

<!-- Or use AsyncRoot / AsyncLogger for selective async -->
<Loggers>
  <AsyncRoot level="INFO">
    <AppenderRef ref="CONSOLE"/>
    <AppenderRef ref="FILE"/>
  </AsyncRoot>
  <AsyncLogger name="com.example" level="DEBUG" additivity="false">
    <AppenderRef ref="CONSOLE"/>
  </AsyncLogger>
</Loggers>

blocks.html: Structured logging emits each log event as a machine-readable JSON object rather than a formatted string. Log aggregation systems (ELK, Loki, Splunk) can then filter, group, and alert on field values without regex parsing. The key enabler is MDC (Mapped Diagnostic Context) — a thread-local key-value map that appenders include in every log event emitted from that thread.

paragraphs:
• MDC.put(key, value): Stores a value in the current thread's context. All subsequent log events from this thread include this key-value pair.
  • MDC.remove(key): Removes a key. Always call in a finally block to prevent context leaks across thread pool reuse.
  • MDC.clear(): Removes all keys. Call at request boundary end.
  • Thread propagation: MDC does not propagate to child threads automatically. Use MDCTaskDecorator (Spring) or Logback's LogbackMDCAdapter to copy context into thread pool workers.

blocks.code:
<!-- pom.xml dependency -->
<dependency>
  <groupId>net.logstash.logback</groupId>
  <artifactId>logstash-logback-encoder</artifactId>
  <version>7.4</version>
</dependency>

<!-- logback-spring.xml appender -->
<appender name="JSON" class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
  <encoder class="net.logstash.logback.encoder.LogstashEncoder">
    <timestampPattern>yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSS'Z'</timestampPattern>
    <timeZone>UTC</timeZone>
    <customFields>{"service":"my-service","team":"platform"}</customFields>
    <!-- MDC fields are included automatically -->
  </encoder>
</appender>

{
  "@timestamp": "2024-01-15T10:23:46.789Z",
  "@version": "1",
  "message": "Payment processed for order 12345",
  "logger_name": "com.example.service.PaymentService",
  "thread_name": "http-nio-8080-exec-1",
  "level": "INFO",
  "level_value": 20000,
  "requestId": "a3f8c2d1-4b5e-4f6a-8c9d-0e1f2a3b4c5d",
  "traceId": "64d9f0a5e3b2c1d0",
  "spanId": "a1b2c3d4e5f60001",
  "userId": "usr_789",
  "service": "my-service",
  "team": "platform"
}

blocks.html: A Kafka log appender routes log events directly to a Kafka topic, bypassing file I/O and log shippers. Log consumers (Logstash, Flink, custom processors) subscribe to the topic and process events in near real-time. This pattern suits high-volume, multi-tenant logging where per-service log files are impractical.

paragraphs:
• Centralized log aggregation: All services write to the same Kafka topic. A single consumer fleet processes and routes events to Elasticsearch, S3, or an alerting system.
  • High-throughput logging: Kafka absorbs log bursts that would overwhelm file-based appenders or Logstash inputs. The topic acts as a buffer.
  • Multi-destination routing: Kafka consumers can replicate events to multiple sinks (ELK, Loki, cold storage) independently.
  • Audit logging: Durable, partitioned, retained topics provide a tamper-resistant audit trail with replay capability.

blocks.code:
<!-- Requires log4j2 (not Logback) -->
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.apache.logging.log4j</groupId>
  <artifactId>log4j-core</artifactId>
</dependency>
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.apache.kafka</groupId>
  <artifactId>kafka-clients</artifactId>
</dependency>

<Appenders>
  <Kafka name="KAFKA" topic="application-logs">
    <JsonLayout compact="true" eventEol="true" includeStacktrace="true"/>
    <Property name="bootstrap.servers">kafka-broker-1:9092,kafka-broker-2:9092</Property>
    <Property name="acks">1</Property>
    <Property name="retries">3</Property>
    <Property name="linger.ms">10</Property>
    <Property name="batch.size">16384</Property>
    <Property name="compression.type">lz4</Property>
  </Kafka>

  <!-- Async wrapper is critical — do not block app threads on Kafka I/O -->
  <Async name="ASYNC_KAFKA" bufferSize="262144">
    <AppenderRef ref="KAFKA"/>
  </Async>
</Appenders>

<dependency>
  <groupId>com.github.danielwegener</groupId>
  <artifactId>logback-kafka-appender</artifactId>
  <version>0.2.0</version>
</dependency>
<dependency>
  <groupId>net.logstash.logback</groupId>
  <artifactId>logstash-logback-encoder</artifactId>
  <version>7.4</version>
</dependency>

<appender name="KAFKA" class="com.github.danielwegener.logback.kafka.KafkaAppender">
  <encoder class="net.logstash.logback.encoder.LogstashEncoder"/>
  <topic>application-logs</topic>
  <keyingStrategy class="com.github.danielwegener.logback.kafka.keying.NoKeyKeyingStrategy"/>
  <deliveryStrategy class="com.github.danielwegener.logback.kafka.delivery.AsynchronousDeliveryStrategy"/>
  <producerConfig>bootstrap.servers=kafka-broker-1:9092,kafka-broker-2:9092</producerConfig>
  <producerConfig>acks=1</producerConfig>
  <producerConfig>linger.ms=10</producerConfig>
  <producerConfig>compression.type=lz4</producerConfig>
  <!-- Fallback appender when Kafka is unavailable -->
  <appender-ref ref="CONSOLE"/>
</appender>

<appender name="ASYNC_KAFKA" class="ch.qos.logback.classic.AsyncAppender">
  <queueSize>512</queueSize>
  <discardingThreshold>0</discardingThreshold>
  <appender-ref ref="KAFKA"/>
</appender>

paragraphs:
• acks=1: Leader acknowledges write. Sufficient for logs — data loss on leader failure is acceptable for non-audit logs.
  • acks=all: All in-sync replicas acknowledge. Use for audit logs where loss is unacceptable. Higher latency.
  • Fallback appender: Configure a console or file appender as fallback. The Logback Kafka appender supports this natively — events route to fallback when Kafka is unreachable.
  • Async wrapper required: Never use a Kafka appender synchronously. Network I/O on the application thread causes latency spikes under load.
  • Key strategy: Use NoKeyKeyingStrategy unless you need ordered delivery for a specific entity — keyed messages go to the same partition, which limits parallelism.

blocks.title: Kafka Appender Pitfalls
blocks.bodyHtml: Log storms: ERROR-level events can flood the Kafka topic during failures. Apply a ThresholdFilter or rate limiter appender filter to cap event rates. Startup race: If Kafka brokers are not available at startup, the appender may block or fail silently. Always configure a fallback appender and verify max.block.ms on the producer config (default 60s — too high for fast startup). Classpath conflict: The Kafka client version in the appender dependency must match or be compatible with the kafka-clients version used by your application. Mismatches cause NoSuchMethodError.

blocks.html: The ELK stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) is the standard log aggregation pipeline for Spring Boot applications. The application emits JSON logs to stdout; Filebeat ships them to Logstash for parsing and enrichment; Logstash indexes them in Elasticsearch; Kibana queries and visualizes them.

blocks.title: ELK Stack Log Pipeline
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid diagram showing the ELK stack log pipeline for a Spring Boot application.

Use `graph LR` (left-to-right) layout.

Nodes in order:
1. `Spring Boot App` (emits JSON logs via LogstashEncoder)
2. → `stdout` (container logs)
3. → `Filebeat` (log shipper, reads container stdout)
4. → `Logstash` (parses, enriches, routes)
5. → `Elasticsearch` (indexes and stores)
6. → `Kibana` (query, visualize, alert)

Alternative path (show with dashed line):
- `Spring Boot App` → `Kafka topic: application-logs` → `Logstash Kafka input` → `Elasticsearch`

Add a side node from `Elasticsearch` → `Alerting` (Kibana alerts or ElastAlert)

Style:
- Group `Filebeat` + `Logstash` in a subgraph labeled `Log Shipping Layer`
- Monochrome, rectangular nodes

blocks.code:
<configuration>
  <springProperty name="appName" source="spring.application.name"/>
  <springProperty name="appVersion" source="app.version" defaultValue="unknown"/>

  <appender name="JSON_STDOUT" class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
    <encoder class="net.logstash.logback.encoder.LogstashEncoder">
      <customFields>{"service":"${appName}","version":"${appVersion}"}</customFields>
      <fieldNames>
        <timestamp>@timestamp</timestamp>
        <message>message</message>
        <logger>logger</logger>
        <thread>thread</thread>
        <level>level</level>
        <levelValue>[ignore]</levelValue>
      </fieldNames>
      <throwableConverter class="net.logstash.logback.stacktrace.ShortenedThrowableConverter">
        <maxDepthPerCause>10</maxDepthPerCause>
        <shortenedClassNameLength>20</shortenedClassNameLength>
      </throwableConverter>
    </encoder>
  </appender>

  <root level="INFO">
    <appender-ref ref="JSON_STDOUT"/>
  </root>
</configuration>

filebeat.inputs:
  - type: container
    paths:
      - /var/lib/docker/containers/*/*.log
    json:
      keys_under_root: true
      overwrite_keys: true
      add_error_key: true
    processors:
      - add_docker_metadata: ~
      - add_kubernetes_metadata: ~

output.logstash:
  hosts: ["logstash:5044"]

# Or send directly to Elasticsearch (skip Logstash)
# output.elasticsearch:
#   hosts: ["elasticsearch:9200"]
#   index: "spring-boot-logs-%{+yyyy.MM.dd}"

paragraphs:
• Use ILM (Index Lifecycle Management): Define hot-warm-cold-delete phases. Hot phase for recent logs with SSD-backed nodes; cold phase for older data on cheaper storage.
  • Index per service per day: spring-logs-my-service-2024.01.15. Simplifies deletion of old data and allows per-service retention policies.
  • Set @timestamp from application log: Use the application's log timestamp, not the ingestion time. Filebeat and Logstash add ingestion delay.
  • Map log levels as keyword: Define an Elasticsearch index template that maps level as keyword for exact-match filtering, not text.
  • Cap stack trace depth: Use ShortenedThrowableConverter in the encoder. Full stack traces in JSON inflate index size significantly.

blocks.html: Grafana Loki is a log aggregation system designed around label-based indexing. Unlike Elasticsearch, Loki indexes only metadata labels (service, pod, level) and stores log content as compressed chunks. Query latency is higher for full-text search, but storage cost is significantly lower. Loki integrates with Grafana for dashboards and alerting, and with Tempo for distributed tracing correlation.

blocks.code:
<dependency>
  <groupId>com.github.loki4j</groupId>
  <artifactId>loki-logback-appender</artifactId>
  <version>1.5.1</version>
</dependency>

# Spring Boot 3.x ships with Loki appender auto-config via Actuator
# No logback-spring.xml needed for basic setup
management:
  otlp:
    logging:
      endpoint: http://loki:3100/otlp/v1/logs

# Or configure Loki push URL directly
logging:
  loki:
    url: http://loki:3100/loki/api/v1/push

<appender name="LOKI" class="com.github.loki4j.logback.Loki4jAppender">
  <http>
    <url>http://loki:3100/loki/api/v1/push</url>
    <!-- Batch up to 1000 events or 1 second, whichever comes first -->
    <batchMaxItems>1000</batchMaxItems>
    <batchTimeoutMs>1000</batchTimeoutMs>
  </http>
  <format>
    <label>
      <!-- Labels are indexed by Loki — keep cardinality low -->
      <pattern>service=${spring.application.name},level=%level,host=${HOSTNAME}</pattern>
    </label>
    <message>
      <!-- Log content stored as compressed chunk -->
      <pattern>%d{yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss.SSS} %-5level [%thread] %logger{36} - %msg%n</pattern>
    </message>
    <sortByTime>true</sortByTime>
  </format>
</appender>

blocks.html: Distributed tracing correlates log events across service boundaries using trace IDs and span IDs. Every log event for a request carries the same traceId, enabling reconstruction of the full request path across microservices. Spring Boot 3.x uses Micrometer Tracing (backed by Brave or OpenTelemetry) to generate and propagate trace context, which is automatically injected into MDC.

blocks.code:
<!-- Micrometer Tracing with Brave (Zipkin-compatible) -->
<dependency>
  <groupId>io.micrometer</groupId>
  <artifactId>micrometer-tracing-bridge-brave</artifactId>
</dependency>
<dependency>
  <groupId>io.zipkin.reporter2</groupId>
  <artifactId>zipkin-reporter-brave</artifactId>
</dependency>

<!-- Or with OpenTelemetry -->
<!-- <dependency>
  <groupId>io.micrometer</groupId>
  <artifactId>micrometer-tracing-bridge-otel</artifactId>
</dependency>
<dependency>
  <groupId>io.opentelemetry</groupId>
  <artifactId>opentelemetry-exporter-otlp</artifactId>
</dependency> -->

blocks.filename: application.yml — trace ID in log pattern
blocks.code:
# Micrometer Tracing injects traceId and spanId into MDC automatically
# Include them in the log pattern:
logging:
  pattern:
    console: "%d{yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss} %-5level [%thread] [%X{traceId}/%X{spanId}] %logger{36} - %msg%n"

# Sampling rate (1.0 = 100%, 0.1 = 10%)
management:
  tracing:
    sampling:
      probability: 1.0
  zipkin:
    tracing:
      endpoint: http://zipkin:9411/api/v2/spans

rows.description: Unique ID for the entire distributed request. Same across all services for one request.
ID for the current operation within the trace. Unique per service hop.
Span ID of the calling service's span. Enables trace tree reconstruction.
Correlation ID from the HTTP request header (X-Request-ID). Client-supplied.
Authenticated user ID. Set from security context after authentication.

blocks.html: Effective logging requires discipline on what to log, at what level, and at what cost. Excessive logging degrades performance; insufficient logging makes incident response impossible. The guidelines below apply regardless of appender stack or log aggregation system.

paragraphs:
• State transitions: Order placed, payment failed, user deactivated. Include entity IDs, not full payloads.
  • External calls: Outbound HTTP, DB queries (at DEBUG), Kafka produce/consume. Log method, target, duration, outcome.
  • Security events: Authentication attempts, authorization failures, privilege escalations. Always at INFO or WARN.
  • Startup and configuration: Active profiles, resolved config sources, server port, DB URL (not password).
  • Errors with context: Exception class, message, relevant entity IDs. Include stack trace for unexpected exceptions; omit for expected ones (validation errors, 404s).

rows.log_when: Per-iteration algorithm steps, SQL bind parameters, HTTP body contents. Never in prod.
Request entry/exit, branch decisions, retry attempts, cache hits/misses.
rows.example: log.debug("Cache miss for key {}, loading from DB", key)

rows.log_when: Business events, state changes, startup milestones, periodic health snapshots.
Degraded behavior, retries, fallbacks, deprecated API usage, slow queries.
rows.example: log.warn("Retry attempt {}/{} for payment {}", attempt, max, paymentId)

rows.log_when: Unrecoverable failures, data corruption, unexpected exceptions, SLA breaches.
rows.example: log.error("Failed to process payment {}: {}", paymentId, e.getMessage(), e)

paragraphs:
• Guard DEBUG and TRACE: Use if (log.isDebugEnabled()) before expensive message construction, or use lambda-based logging: log.debug(() -> "State: " + expensiveComputation()).
  • Avoid string concatenation in log calls: Use SLF4J's {} placeholders. The string is only constructed if the log level is enabled.
  • Async appenders: File and Kafka appenders should always be wrapped in an async appender. Synchronous disk I/O on request threads adds latency and increases tail latency.
  • Sampling high-volume events: For per-request TRACE logging at high throughput, implement a TurboFilter that samples a percentage of events rather than logging all.

blocks.title: Logging Anti-Patterns
blocks.bodyHtml: Logging and rethrowing: catch (Exception e) { log.error(..., e); throw e; } — the same exception gets logged multiple times as it propagates. Log at the boundary where you handle it, not every layer. Swallowing exceptions: catch (Exception e) { log.error("Error"); } — no stack trace, no context. Always include the exception object as the last argument to get a stack trace. Using System.out.println: Bypasses the logging framework, no level control, no MDC, no structured output. Logging entire request/response bodies: Bloats log storage, risks leaking PII, and degrades performance. Log entity IDs and outcome codes instead.

paragraphs:
• Container stdout only (Kubernetes/ECS): Use LogstashEncoder to console stdout. The orchestrator captures and ships it. No file appender needed.
  • Need full-text search on log content: ELK stack. Logstash for parsing, Elasticsearch for indexing, Kibana for queries and dashboards.
  • Cost-sensitive, Grafana already in use: Loki. Lower storage cost, good label-based filtering, native Grafana integration. Not suitable as primary search for unknown patterns.
  • High-volume audit logging with replay requirement: Kafka appender → Kafka topic → downstream consumers. Kafka provides durable, replayable, partitioned storage.
  • Distributed tracing correlation required: Add Micrometer Tracing. traceId/spanId automatically injected into MDC. Correlate logs in Kibana or Loki with traces in Zipkin, Tempo, or Jaeger.
  • Async performance is critical: Log4j2 with Disruptor-backed async loggers. Better throughput than Logback's AsyncAppender under sustained load.
